content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement
(916) 322-5662          322-5660         322-5901         322-6441

August 16, 1985


Dee Gipson
DRR Investments
P.O. Box 244
Riverside, CA 95216

Re: Major Donor Filing Requirement
    of Duane R. Roberts
    FPPC Advice No. A-85-133

Dear Ms. Gipson:

This is in reply to your letter dated May 23, 1985, which recently was referred to us by the Secretary of State's Political Reform Division.

The facts as I understand them from your letter are that Duane Roberts made a payment of $10,000 to purchase tickets to a campaign fundraiser for Governor Deukmejian. Mr. Roberts was to be reimbursed for $5,000 by other people who attended the fundraiser with him. In fact, at the time of your second inquiry to the Secretary of State, Mr. Roberts had received $5,000 from four people.

You asked whether Mr. Roberts qualified as a committee under Gov. Code Section 82013(c) -- a "major donor committee" -- because of the $10,000 contribution.

The Commission Staff has determined that when a person purchases tickets to a fundraising event, with the understanding that all or a portion of the cost of the tickets will be reimbursed by others, the person is not required to report the amount that will be reimbursed (and that amount does not count toward qualifying the person as a "committee"), as long as the person who purchased the tickets receives the reimbursement no later than the date of the fundraising event, or within 30 days of purchasing the tickets.

If payment is not received within this time period, the person must aggregate the full cost of the fundraising tickets with all other contributions he or she has made to determine whether he or she meets the definition of a committee and must file reports. The entire payment must be reported on the campaign statement required to be filed, and a note should be made to explain that the filer expects to receive reimbursement for a portion of the costs. At such time as the person receives reimbursement, a notation must be made on the next campaign statement, if any, filed for that calendar year to reflect the reimbursement.

Dee Gipson
August 16, 1985
Page 2


     In addition, it is important to note that, at the time the
tickets are purchased, the person making the payment must inform the
candidate or committee on whose behalf the fundraiser is held that he
or she is acting as an intermediary for others, and must provide the
candidate or committee with the name, address, occupation and amount
of contribution, of each person who made a commitment to purchase any
portion of the tickets. If payment is not received pursuant to the
time period described above, the person who made the payment must
inform the candidate or committee that the entire contribution is
from the person who made the payment. In such a case, the candidate
or committee must amend its campaign disclosure statement to indicate
the person who made the payment as the contributor for the entire
amount.

     Under the circumstances described in your letter, if Mr. Roberts
received payment from the four people who attended the fundraiser
with him no later than the date of the fundraising event or no later
than 30 days after making the payment, he was not required to file a
campaign statement as a major donor, since his own contributions did
not total $10,000 or more.

     Please call me if you have any questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Technical Assistance and
                                        Analysis Division

JP:kt

cc:  Wayne Imberi
     Political Reform Division
     Secretary of State

LL PAUL
D.R.R. INVESTMENTS

May 23, 1985

Second Request

Political Reform Division
P. O. box 1467
Sacramento, California 95807

RE: Reimbursement on a Contribution

To Whom It May Concern:

On the enclosed threshhold report, you will note on 5/6/85, Mr. Roberts made a $10,000 contribution for a fund raiser to re-elect Governor George Deukmejian.

Of this $10,000 contribution, Mr. Roberts is being reimbursed $5,000 by others who are attending with him and will be at his table.

Our question is--how does this affect Mr. Roberts' major donor contribution form? He will really only be donating $5,000 since he will be receiving $5,000 back from those who attended with him.

An answer would greatly be appreciated so we will know how to handle the next report. As we are reimbursed, I will keep track and forward those dates and names for your reference (with next threshold report, unless you need earlier).

Thanking you in advance for your response.

Sincerely,

Dee Gipson
Executive Secretary to
Duane R. Roberts

Enclosure: Threshold report - March 18, 1985 to May 18, 1985

Reimbursement
6/7 - Kenneth Calvert      $1,000
6/3 - John King            $1,000
6/14 - Albert Stibes       $2,000
6/14 - [illegible]         $1,000
                           $5,000 reimbursed to

expenditures on behalf of candidates, officeholders or ballot measures
totaling $500 or more in a calendar year.

sections.question_synthetic: Does a person who purchases campaign fundraiser tickets for $10,000 but expects to be reimbursed $5,000 by others qualify as a major donor committee under California law?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A person purchasing fundraiser tickets with expected reimbursement from others is not required to count the reimbursed amount toward major donor status if reimbursement is received by the fundraiser date or within 30 days of purchase. If reimbursement is not timely received, the full ticket cost must be reported and aggregated with other contributions to determine filing obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains original incoming request letter (dated May 23, 1985) appended to FPPC response letter (dated August 16, 1985). Also includes Form 461 (Major Donor Committee Campaign Statement). Clear Q&A structure with detailed conclusion and analysis of reimbursement timing and disclosure requirements.

QUESTION: You asked whether Mr. Roberts qualified as a committee under Gov. Code Section 82013(c) -- a "major donor committee" -- because of the $10,000 contribution.

CONCLUSION: The Commission Staff has determined that when a person purchases tickets to a fundraising event, with the understanding that all or a portion of the cost of the tickets will be reimbursed by others, the person is not required to report the amount that will be reimbursed (and that amount does not count toward qualifying the person as a "committee"), as long as the person who purchased the tickets receives the reimbursement no later than the date of the fundraising event, or within 30 days of purchasing the tickets. If payment is not received within this time period, the person must aggregate the full cost of the fundraising tickets with all other contributions he or she has made to determine whether he or she meets the definition of a committee and must file reports.

The facts as I understand them from your letter are that Duane Roberts made a payment of $10,000 to purchase tickets to a campaign fundraiser for Governor Deukmejian. Mr. Roberts was to be reimbursed for $5,000 by other people who attended the fundraiser with him. In fact, at the time of your second inquiry to the Secretary of State, Mr. Roberts had received $5,000 from four people. You asked whether Mr. Roberts qualified as a committee under Gov. Code Section 82013(c) -- a "major donor committee" -- because of the $10,000 contribution. The Commission Staff has determined that when a person purchases tickets to a fundraising event, with the understanding that all or a portion of the cost of the tickets will be reimbursed by others, the person is not required to report the amount that will be reimbursed (and that amount does not count toward qualifying the person as a "committee"), as long as the person who purchased the tickets receives the reimbursement no later than the date of the fundraising event, or within 30 days of purchasing the tickets. If payment is not received within this time period, the person must aggregate the full cost of the fundraising tickets with all other contributions he or she has made to determine whether he or she meets the definition of a committee and must file reports. The entire payment must be reported on the campaign statement required to be filed, and a note should be made to explain that the filer expects to receive reimbursement for a portion of the costs. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether Duane Roberts' $10,000 campaign fundraiser ticket purchase, with expected $5,000 reimbursement from attendees, qualifies him as a major donor committee. The Commission determined that timely reimbursements need not be counted toward major donor status, but established detailed disclosure requirements and notification procedures for intermediary ticket purchases.
